title: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)
items.title: Identifying Heart Murmurs by Timing
items.content: Heart murmurs are classified based on their timing within the cardiac cycle, which is crucial for initial identification. Systolic murmurs occur from or after the first heart sound (S1) and end at or before the second heart sound (S2). Diastolic murmurs begin with or after S2 and end at or before the next S1. Continuous murmurs span both systole and diastole.
keyPoints: Systolic murmurs: Begin with or after S1, end at or before S2.
Diastolic murmurs: Begin with or after S2, end at or before S1.
Continuous murmurs: Span both systole and diastole.
Distinguishing heart sounds can be challenging in tachyarrhythmia; use carotid palpation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)
items.title: Characteristics of Heart Murmurs
items.content: The duration and character of heart murmurs are influenced by pressure differences between cardiac chambers or vessels, flow velocity, turbulence, and chamber geometry. Diastolic murmurs in chronic AR are high-frequency and blowing, while mitral stenosis (MS) presents as a low-frequency rumble. The frequency components can vary at different auscultation sites.
keyPoints: Duration depends on pressure differences between chambers or vessels.
Diastolic murmur of chronic AR: High-frequency, blowing sound.
Mitral stenosis (MS): Low-frequency, rumbling sound.
Frequency components may vary by auscultation site.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)
items.title: Murmur Configuration and Clinical Implications
items.content: Heart murmurs can be described as crescendo, decrescendo, crescendo-decrescendo, or plateau. The decrescendo murmur of chronic AR reflects a declining diastolic pressure gradient between the aorta and left ventricle. AS murmurs have a crescendo-decrescendo pattern due to changes in systolic pressure gradients during ejection. Chronic MR murmurs are plateau-shaped, indicating a constant pressure difference.
keyPoints: Murmur configurations: Crescendo, decrescendo, crescendo-decrescendo, plateau.
Chronic AR murmur: Decrescendo due to declining diastolic pressure gradient.
Aortic stenosis (AS) murmur: Crescendo-decrescendo from systolic pressure changes.
Chronic MR murmur: Plateau configuration with constant pressure difference.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 2)
items.title: Grading Intensity of Heart Murmurs
items.content: The intensity of a heart murmur is graded on a scale from 1 to 6, which helps in assessing the severity and potential underlying structural heart disease. Higher-grade murmurs often indicate significant pathology.
keyPoints: Murmur intensity grading: 1 (very soft) to 6 (loud with stethoscope off chest).
Grade 3 or greater usually signifies important structural heart disease due to high blood flow velocity at the murmur site.
Example: Small, restrictive VSDs produce loud murmurs of grade 4 or higher.
Murmur intensity can be affected by factors such as obesity, obstructive lung disease, large pericardial effusion, reduced cardiac output, and low pressure gradients.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 2)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 2)
items.title: Importance of Location and Radiation in Identifying Heart Murmurs
items.content: Identifying the location and radiation of heart murmurs is crucial for accurate diagnosis. Additional clues from adventitious sounds and changes during respiratory cycles aid in distinguishing between different types of murmurs.
keyPoints: Location and radiation help accurately identify heart murmurs.
Adventitious sounds (e.g., systolic click, diastolic snap) provide additional diagnostic clues.
Assessing murmur characteristics during the respiratory cycle aids diagnosis.
Bedside maneuvers complete the auscultatory examination and guide further testing.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 2)

items.title: Acute Severe Mitral Regurgitation: Clinical Presentation and Imaging
items.content: Acute severe mitral regurgitation (MR) can occur due to papillary muscle rupture, chordae tendineae rupture, infective endocarditis, or blunt chest trauma. It presents with early systolic murmur best heard at the apical impulse, often accompanied by chest pain, hypotension, and pulmonary edema. A new heart murmur post-myocardial infarction necessitates transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) to determine etiology.
keyPoints: Early systolic murmurs begin with S1 and end before S2.
Acute severe MR occurs 2–7 days after myocardial infarction, often involving the posteromedial papillary muscle 6–10 times more frequently than the anterolateral.
Up to 50% of cases may present without a murmur.
TTE is indicated for suspected acute severe MR to define mechanism and severity.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.title: Differentiating Acute Mitral Regurgitation from Ventricular Septal Rupture
items.content: Acute mitral regurgitation and ventricular septal rupture can both follow myocardial infarction. Differentiation is crucial: acute MR presents with an early decrescendo murmur, while ventricular septal rupture has a holosystolic murmur with a systolic thrill at the left sternal border. Right-sided heart catheterization and analysis of pressure waveforms aid in differentiation.
keyPoints: Acute MR is characterized by an early decrescendo murmur, unlike the holosystolic murmur of ventricular septal rupture.
Ventricular septal rupture often presents with a systolic thrill at the left sternal border.
Right-sided heart catheterization and pressure waveform analysis can differentiate between the two conditions.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.title: Congenital Small Muscular VSD: Characteristics and Diagnosis
items.content: A congenital small muscular ventricular septal defect (VSD) presents with an early systolic murmur localized to the left sternal border, typically grade 4 or 5 in intensity. It is confined to early systole due to progressive closure during septal contraction. TTE is indicated for suspected VSD to assess severity and pulmonary hypertension.
keyPoints: Early systolic murmurs from small muscular VSDs are localized to the left sternal border and grade 4 or 5.
The murmur is confined to early systole due to septal contraction.
TTE is indicated for suspected VSD to assess severity and pulmonary hypertension.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.title: Differentiating Aortic Stenosis from Mitral Regurgitation
items.content: Aortic stenosis (AS) and mitral regurgitation (MR) can present with similar midsystolic murmurs. AS murmurs increase in intensity after a premature beat, whereas MR murmurs maintain constant intensity. Additionally, AS murmur intensity correlates with cardiac output.
keyPoints: AS murmur increases in intensity after a premature beat; MR does not change.
AS murmur intensity varies with cardiac output.
Severe AS is suggested by a systolic thrill at the second right intercostal space and a grade 4 or higher murmur.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.title: Assessing Aortic Stenosis Severity
items.content: In AS, severe stenosis is indicated by a parvus et tardus carotid pulse and auscultatory findings such as a soft or absent A2, paradoxical splitting of S2, an apical S4, and a late-peaking systolic murmur. TTE assesses the severity of stenosis and related cardiac features.
keyPoints: Parvus et tardus carotid pulse suggests severe AS.
Auscultatory signs: soft/absent A2, paradoxical S2 splitting, apical S4, late-peaking murmur.
TTE evaluates aortic valve anatomy, stenosis severity, and left ventricular characteristics.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.title: Characteristics of Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy Murmur
items.content: Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM) presents with a midsystolic murmur that varies with maneuvers affecting outflow tract obstruction. It increases with reduced preload or afterload and decreases with increased preload or afterload.
keyPoints: HOCM murmur loudest along left sternal border or between lower sternal border and apex.
Increases with Valsalva, standing; decreases with squatting, volume administration.
Associated findings: rapid carotid upstroke, LVH on ECG.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.title: When to Evaluate Systolic Murmurs with TTE
items.content: TTE is indicated for evaluation of a grade 2 or 3 midsystolic murmur when there are other signs of cardiac disease. An isolated grade 1 or 2 midsystolic murmur without symptoms or signs of heart disease is often benign and does not require further evaluation.
keyPoints: TTE indicated for grade 2 or 3 midsystolic murmurs with additional cardiac signs
Isolated grade 1 or 2 midsystolic murmurs are usually benign without further evaluation needed
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 3)

items.title: Characteristics and Maneuvers for Late Systolic Murmurs
items.content: Late systolic murmurs at the left ventricular apex are typically due to MVP, characterized by clicks and specific radiation patterns. Maneuvers affecting preload can alter the timing and intensity of these murmurs.
keyPoints: MVP causes late systolic murmurs with nonejection clicks
Radiation helps identify prolapsed mitral leaflet (anterior or posterior)
Standing decreases preload, making murmur louder and closer to S1; squatting increases preload, delaying murmur
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 3)

items.title: Holosystolic Murmurs Indicative of Regurgitation or VSD
items.content: Holosystolic murmurs, which start with S1 and continue to S2, suggest chronic mitral or tricuspid regurgitation or a ventricular septal defect (VSD). TTE is recommended for further evaluation.
keyPoints: Holosystolic murmurs indicate chronic MR, TR, or VSD
Best heard at the left ventricular apex and radiates to axilla in chronic MR
TTE recommended for characterization of holosystolic murmurs
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 3)

items.title: Chronic Mitral Regurgitation: Mechanisms and Progression
items.content: Chronic mitral regurgitation (MR) is associated with conditions like rheumatic scarring, mitral annular calcification, postinfarction remodeling, and dilated cardiomyopathy. These factors contribute to apical holosystolic murmurs by causing papillary muscle displacement and leaflet tethering. Chronic MR leads to left atrial enlargement, which stretches the mitral annulus further, worsening MR in a self-perpetuating cycle.
keyPoints: Conditions associated with chronic MR include rheumatic scarring, mitral annular calcification, postinfarction remodeling, and dilated cardiomyopathy.
Apical displacement of papillary muscles and leaflet tethering worsen MR severity.
Chronic severe MR leads to left ventricular apex beat enlargement and possible diastolic filling complex.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 4)

items.title: Tricuspid Regurgitation: Clinical Features and Causes
items.content: The holosystolic murmur of chronic tricuspid regurgitation (TR) is softer than MR, loudest at the left lower sternal border, and intensifies with inspiration (Carvallo's sign). Signs include c-v waves in jugular venous pulse, enlarged pulsatile liver, ascites, and peripheral edema. TR can be primary due to conditions like myxomatous disease or secondary from right ventricular dilation. Cardiac device leads may cause TR through leaflet trauma.
keyPoints: TR murmur is softer than MR, loudest at left lower sternal border, increases with inspiration (Carvallo's sign).
Signs: c-v waves in jugular venous pulse, enlarged pulsatile liver, ascites, peripheral edema.
Primary TR causes include myxomatous disease, endocarditis, rheumatic disease; secondary from right ventricular dilation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 4)

items.title: Characteristics and Auscultation of Chronic Aortic Regurgitation Murmur
items.content: Chronic aortic regurgitation (AR) is identified by a high-pitched, blowing, decrescendo early to mid-diastolic murmur that begins after the aortic component of S2. It's best heard at the second right interspace and along the left sternal border. Auscultation is optimized when the patient leans forward at end expiration.
keyPoints: High-pitched, blowing, decrescendo early to mid-diastolic murmur
Begins after aortic component of S2 (A2)
Best heard at second right interspace and left sternal border
Auscultation improved by leaning forward at end expiration
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.title: Differentiating Austin Flint Murmur from Mitral Stenosis
items.content: The Austin Flint murmur, associated with severe AR, is a lower-pitched mid to late diastolic murmur at the apex. It differs from mitral stenosis by lacking an opening snap and responding to vasodilator challenge, where it becomes shorter and softer.
keyPoints: Lower-pitched mid to late diastolic murmur
Located at the apex (Austin Flint murmur)
Absence of opening snap differentiates from mitral stenosis
Murmur shortens and softens with vasodilator challenge
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.title: Indications for TTE in Diastolic Murmurs
items.content: Transesophageal echocardiography (TTE) is indicated for further evaluation of patients with early to mid-diastolic murmurs. It assesses lesion severity, ventricular size, and systolic function, aiding surgical management decisions.
keyPoints: Indicated for evaluation of early to mid-diastolic murmurs
Assesses lesion severity, ventricular size, and systolic function
Guides potential decision for surgical management
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.title: Characteristics of Mitral Stenosis Murmurs
items.content: Mitral stenosis (MS) murmurs are low-pitched, best heard with the bell of the stethoscope at the left ventricular apex. They begin after an opening snap and increase in intensity during maneuvers that elevate cardiac output.
keyPoints: Opening snap occurs shortly after S2, inversely related to left atrial-left ventricular pressure gradient.
Murmur is loudest at the left ventricular apex; often appreciated only when patient is in left lateral decubitus position.
Intensity increases with maneuvers that increase cardiac output and mitral valve flow (e.g., exercise).
Presystolic accentuation occurs just before S1 in sinus rhythm due to late transmitral flow increase with atrial contraction.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.title: Tricuspid Stenosis Murmur Identification
items.content: The mid-diastolic murmur of tricuspid stenosis is best heard at the lower left sternal border and increases with inspiration. It's often difficult to hear due to overlapping left-sided acoustical events.
keyPoints: Best heard at lower left sternal border, intensity increases with inspiration.
Prolonged y descent may be visible in jugular venous waveform.
Murmur is challenging to detect and often obscured by other heart sounds.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.title: Evaluating Mid-Diastolic Murmurs with TTE
items.content: TTE is recommended for evaluating patients with mid- to late diastolic murmurs, as it helps identify specific disease characteristics and guide management.
keyPoints: TTE indicated for evaluation of mid- to late diastolic murmurs.
Findings will help differentiate causes such as MS, tricuspid stenosis, myxomas, AR, MR, ASDs, and TR.
Management is guided by specific disease characteristics identified via TTE.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.title: Continuous Murmurs: Indicative of Persistent Pressure Gradients
items.content: Continuous murmurs, which span both systole and diastole, suggest a persistent pressure gradient between two cardiac chambers or vessels. This can be due to conditions like patent ductus arteriosus (PDA), ruptured sinus of Valsalva aneurysm, coronary arteriovenous fistula, and others.
keyPoints: Continuous murmurs span both systole and diastole.
Indicate a pressure gradient between two chambers or vessels during the cardiac cycle.
PDA murmur is best heard lateral to the upper left sternal border.
Ruptured sinus of Valsalva aneurysm presents with a continuous murmur at the upper right sternal border.
Continuous murmurs may also be associated with coronary arteriovenous fistula, hemodialysis access sites, and aortic coarctation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · CONTINUOUS MURMURS

items.title: Non-Pathologic Continuous Murmurs: Recognizing Benign Causes
items.content: Not all continuous murmurs are indicative of pathology. Some, like the venous hum and mammary souffle, occur in healthy individuals under specific conditions such as respiration or pregnancy.
keyPoints: Continuous venous hum is common in children/young adults; best heard in right supraclavicular fossa.
Venous hum can be obliterated by pressure over the internal jugular vein or head turning.
Mammary souffle occurs during late pregnancy, louder in systole, and can have its diastolic portion eliminated with stethoscope pressure.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · CONTINUOUS MURMURS

items.title: Respiration and Heart Murmurs: Auscultation Techniques
items.content: Heart murmurs can be characterized by their behavior during respiration. Left-sided murmurs are best heard at end expiration, while right-sided murmurs increase in intensity with inspiration.
keyPoints: Left-sided murmurs may be best heard at end expiration due to minimized lung volumes and closer proximity of the heart to the chest wall.
Right-sided murmurs (e.g., tricuspid or pulmonic regurgitation) increase in intensity during inspiration.
The intensity of left-sided murmurs either remains constant or decreases with inspiration.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DYNAMIC AUSCULTATION

items.title: Systemic Vascular Resistance and Murmur Characteristics
items.content: Murmur characteristics change with maneuvers that alter systemic vascular resistance. Systolic murmurs of MR and VSD become louder, while AS or HOCM murmurs soften.
keyPoints: Sustained handgrip or inflation of blood pressure cuffs increases systolic murmurs of MR and VSD.
AS or HOCM murmurs become softer with increased systemic vascular resistance.
Diastolic murmur of AR becomes louder when systemic vascular resistance is raised.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DYNAMIC AUSCULTATION

items.title: Post-Premature Ventricular Contraction and Murmur Intensity
items.content: The intensity change in a systolic murmur after a premature beat can help distinguish AS from MR. AS murmurs increase due to enhanced filling and contractile function.
keyPoints: AS murmurs increase in intensity after a premature beat due to increased left ventricular filling and post-extrasystolic potentiation.
MR murmur intensity does not change significantly after a premature beat.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DYNAMIC AUSCULTATION

items.title: When to Perform Echocardiography for Heart Murmurs
items.content: Echocardiography is indicated based on the type and grade of heart murmur, as well as accompanying clinical signs or symptoms. It is essential for evaluating early, late, holosystolic murmurs, grade 3 or louder midsystolic murmurs, diastolic murmurs, continuous murmurs not due to benign causes, and when there's a need to verify normal cardiac structure in patients with noncardiac symptoms.
keyPoints: Indicated for early, late, holosystolic murmurs, grade 3 or louder midsystolic murmurs
Also indicated for diastolic murmurs and continuous murmurs not due to venous hum or mammary souffle
Considered when verifying normal cardiac structure in patients with noncardiac symptoms
Not recommended routinely for asymptomatic patients with grade 1 or 2 midsystolic murmur without other signs of heart disease
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · THE CLINICAL CONTEXT | ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY

items.title: Clinical Context Dictates Need for Further Testing in Heart Murmurs
items.content: The clinical context, including symptoms and physical examination findings, helps determine the need for further testing of heart murmurs. For example, a new systolic murmur post-MI with hypotension suggests myocardial rupture, whereas an isolated grade 1 or 2 midsystolic murmur in a healthy young adult is likely benign.
keyPoints: New systolic murmur post-MI with hypotension may suggest myocardial rupture
Isolated grade 1 or 2 midsystolic murmur in asymptomatic, active young adults is likely benign
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · THE CLINICAL CONTEXT | ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY

items.title: Role of Doppler Signals and Operator-Dependent Devices in Murmur Evaluation
items.content: Doppler signals can detect trace or mild valvular regurgitation, which may not be clinically significant. Handheld cardiac ultrasound devices have improved sensitivity for detecting valvular heart disease but are highly operator-dependent and lack comprehensive cost-effectiveness analysis.
keyPoints: Trace or mild valvular regurgitation detected by Doppler may not create audible murmurs
Handheld cardiac ultrasound devices show improved sensitivity but are operator-dependent
Cost-effectiveness of handheld devices has not been rigorously analyzed
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · THE CLINICAL CONTEXT | ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY

items.title: When to Consider Transesophageal Echocardiography (TEE) for Heart Murmurs
items.content: Transesophageal echocardiography is recommended when transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) fails to adequately characterize a heart murmur due to limitations such as body size, chest configuration, or intrathoracic pathology. TEE provides enhanced sensitivity for detecting structural cardiac disorders.
keyPoints: TEE is considered when TTE windows are limited by body size, chest configuration, or intrathoracic pathology.
TEE offers enhanced sensitivity for the detection of a wide range of structural cardiac disorders.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · OTHER CARDIAC TESTING | INTEGRATED APPROACH

items.title: Role of Cardiac Magnetic Resonance (CMR) Imaging in Heart Murmurs
items.content: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging is used to provide quantitative information on valvular function, regurgitant fraction, and other cardiac parameters. It has largely replaced the need for invasive hemodynamic assessment when there's a discrepancy between clinical and echocardiographic findings in patients with regurgitant heart valve disease.
keyPoints: CMR imaging provides quantitative data on valvular function, regurgitant fraction, regurgitant volume, shunt flow, chamber size, ventricular function, and myocardial perfusion.
CMR has largely supplanted the need for cardiac catheterization in cases of discrepancy between clinical and echocardiographic findings in patients with regurgitant heart valve disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · OTHER CARDIAC TESTING | INTEGRATED APPROACH

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)
items.question: Which of the following best describes the timing of systolic murmurs?
options: Begin after the second heart sound and end before the first heart sound.
Begin with or after the first heart sound and terminate at or before the component of the second heart sound that corresponds to their site of origin.
Begin in early diastole and proceed through the first heart sound into all or part of systole.
Are not confined to either phase of the cardiac cycle.
items.explanation: Systolic murmurs begin with or after S1 and terminate at or before A2 or P2, corresponding to their site of origin.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)
items.question: What is the characteristic sound quality of the diastolic murmur in chronic aortic regurgitation?
options: Honking sound due to mitral valve prolapse.
items.explanation: The diastolic murmur of chronic aortic regurgitation is described as blowing and high-frequency.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)
items.question: Which maneuver can help distinguish heart sounds in the setting of tachyarrhythmia?
options: Simultaneous palpation of the carotid upstroke.
Using a stethoscope with higher frequency settings.
items.explanation: In tachyarrhythmia, simultaneous palpation of the carotid upstroke helps distinguish heart sounds by following S1.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)
items.question: What is the configuration of the murmur in chronic mitral regurgitation?
items.explanation: The murmur of chronic mitral regurgitation has a plateau configuration due to the large and nearly constant pressure difference between the left ventricle and the left atrium.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 2)
items.question: Which grade murmur is loud enough to be heard with only the edge of the stethoscope touching the chest?
items.explanation: A grade 5 murmur is loud enough to be heard with only the edge of the stethoscope touching the chest.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 2)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 2)
items.question: Which condition is typically associated with a low-velocity event and usually silent murmur?
items.explanation: Left-to-right shunting across an atrial septal defect (ASD) is typically associated with a low-velocity event and usually silent murmur.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 2)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 2)
items.question: What can diminish the intensity of a heart murmur?
options: Decreased distance between intracardiac source and stethoscope
High pressure gradient between involved cardiac structures
items.explanation: The intensity of a heart murmur may be diminished by any process that increases the distance between the intracardiac source and the stethoscope on the chest wall, such as obesity.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 2)

items.question: Which clinical setting is most frequently associated with acute severe mitral regurgitation due to papillary muscle rupture?
options: Superior, anterior or septal myocardial infarction
Inferior, posterior or lateral myocardial infarction
items.explanation: Acute severe MR from papillary muscle rupture usually accompanies an inferior, posterior, or lateral MI.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.question: What is a distinguishing feature of the murmur associated with post-MI ventricular septal rupture compared to acute MR?
options: It is accompanied by a systolic thrill at the left sternal border
It is best heard at or just medial to the apical impulse
items.explanation: The murmur associated with post-MI ventricular septal rupture is accompanied by a systolic thrill at the left sternal border.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.question: What diagnostic tool is indicated for suspected acute severe MR to delineate its mechanism and severity?
items.explanation: TTE is indicated in all cases of suspected acute, severe MR to define its mechanism and severity.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.question: Which murmur characteristic helps differentiate a congenital small muscular VSD from other conditions?
options: Confined to early systole due to progressive closure during septal contraction
Associated with signs of pulmonary hypertension
Best heard at the lower left sternal border
items.explanation: The murmur is confined to early systole as the defect closes progressively during septal contraction.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.question: Which murmur is usually loudest to the right of the sternum in the second intercostal space?
items.explanation: The murmur of aortic stenosis is usually loudest to the right of the sternum in the second intercostal space (aortic area).
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.question: What maneuver increases the intensity of the murmur associated with hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy?
items.explanation: The Valsalva maneuver increases the intensity of the murmur associated with hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy by increasing outflow tract obstruction.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.question: Which finding is consistent with severe aortic stenosis?
options: Rapid and normal volume carotid upstroke
Small and delayed carotid pulse upstroke
Increased intensity of murmur after squatting
items.explanation: A small and delayed upstroke (parvus et tardus) is consistent with severe aortic stenosis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.question: Where is the midsystolic murmur of congenital pulmonic stenosis best appreciated?
options: Second and third left intercostal spaces
items.explanation: The midsystolic murmur of congenital pulmonic stenosis is best appreciated in the second and third left intercostal spaces (pulmonic area).
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.question: Which feature is suggestive of a primum ASD?
options: Coexistence of MR due to a cleft anterior mitral valve leaflet and left axis deviation of the QRS complex
Large left-to-right shunt resulting in a systolic murmur
Pulmonary ejection sound at the upper left sternal border
Normal carotid upstrokes with electrocardiographic LVH
items.explanation: Primum ASD is suggested by MR due to a cleft anterior mitral valve leaflet and left axis deviation of the QRS complex.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 3)

items.question: Which condition often presents with an isolated grade 1 or 2 midsystolic murmur in older adults?
options: Idiopathic dilation of the pulmonary artery
items.explanation: An isolated grade 1 or 2 midsystolic murmur in older adults is often due to aortic valve sclerosis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 3)

items.question: What maneuver will cause the click and murmur of MVP to move closer to the first heart sound?
items.explanation: Standing decreases left ventricular preload, causing the click and murmur of MVP to move closer to the first heart sound.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 3)

items.question: What is a common feature of holosystolic murmurs?
options: They begin with S1 and continue through systole to S2
They are indicative of aortic stenosis
They decrease in intensity during squatting
They are best heard at the second right interspace
items.explanation: Holosystolic murmurs begin with S1 and continue through systole to S2.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 3)

items.question: Which condition is associated with chronic mitral regurgitation (MR) and an apical holosystolic murmur?
items.explanation: Chronic MR with an apical holosystolic murmur can be associated with rheumatic scarring of the leaflets.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 4)

items.question: Which sign is characteristic of tricuspid regurgitation (TR) and involves an increase in murmur intensity during inspiration?
items.explanation: Carvallo's sign is characterized by an increase in the intensity of the TR murmur with inspiration.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 4)

items.question: What is a common finding in patients with chronic severe mitral regurgitation?
options: Enlargement and leftward displacement of the apex beat
Pulmonary hypertension without right heart failure
items.explanation: Chronic severe MR can lead to enlargement and leftward displacement of the left ventricular apex beat.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 4)

items.question: Which maneuver is recommended to better hear an early diastolic murmur due to chronic AR?
items.explanation: Leaning forward at end expiration brings the aortic root closer to the anterior chest wall, making it easier to hear an early diastolic murmur.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.question: Where does the Graham Steell murmur of pulmonic regurgitation best radiate?
items.explanation: The Graham Steell murmur is best heard at the second left interspace and radiates along the left sternal border.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.question: What characterizes the diastolic murmur of acute severe AR compared to chronic AR?
options: It is higher pitched and longer in duration.
It is lower pitched and shorter in duration.
It radiates along the right sternal border.
items.explanation: The diastolic murmur of acute severe AR is notably shorter in duration and lower pitched than that of chronic AR.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.question: What effect does a vasodilator challenge have on the Austin Flint murmur?
options: It increases the duration and magnitude.
It decreases the duration and magnitude.
It has no effect on the murmur.
It causes the murmur to disappear completely.
items.explanation: Lowering afterload with a vasodilator like amyl nitrite will decrease the duration and magnitude of the Austin Flint murmur in severe AR.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is true about the murmur associated with mitral stenosis?
options: It begins after an opening snap and is best heard at the left ventricular apex.
It is a high-pitched murmur that increases in intensity during expiration.
The murmur has presystolic accentuation in patients with atrial fibrillation.
It is loudest at the lower left sternal border.
items.explanation: Mitral stenosis murmur begins after an opening snap and is best heard at the left ventricular apex.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.question: What characterizes presystolic accentuation in a mid-diastolic murmur of mitral stenosis?
options: It occurs due to increased transmitral flow with atrial contraction.
It is absent in patients with sinus rhythm.
It happens when the cardiac output is severely reduced.
items.explanation: Presystolic accentuation occurs due to increased transmitral flow with atrial contraction and is seen in patients with sinus rhythm.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.question: Where is the mid-diastolic murmur associated with tricuspid stenosis best heard?
options: At the lower left sternal border.
At the upper right sternal border.
items.explanation: The mid-diastolic murmur associated with tricuspid stenosis is best heard at the lower left sternal border.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.question: What does a prolonged y descent in the jugular venous waveform indicate?
items.explanation: A prolonged y descent in the jugular venous waveform may indicate increased tricuspid valve flow, as seen with tricuspid stenosis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.question: Where is the continuous murmur associated with a patent ductus arteriosus best heard?
options: Lateral to the upper left sternal border
items.explanation: The continuous murmur of a patent ductus arteriosus is best heard lateral to the upper left sternal border.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · CONTINUOUS MURMURS

items.question: What condition can lead to attenuation or obliteration of the diastolic component of a continuous murmur?
items.explanation: Large, uncorrected shunts may lead to pulmonary hypertension, causing attenuation or obliteration of the diastolic component.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · CONTINUOUS MURMURS

items.question: What is a common non-pathologic cause of continuous murmurs in healthy children and young adults?
items.explanation: A continuous venous hum is a non-pathologic cause commonly heard in healthy children and young adults.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · CONTINUOUS MURMURS

items.question: During which phase of respiration are left-sided murmurs typically best heard?
options: End inspiration, when lung volumes are maximized.
End expiration, when lung volumes are minimized.
Throughout the respiratory cycle without change.
items.explanation: Left-sided murmurs may be best heard at end expiration because lung volumes are minimized and the heart is closer to the chest wall.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DYNAMIC AUSCULTATION

items.question: What happens to right-sided murmurs during inspiration?
items.explanation: Right-sided murmurs, such as tricuspid or pulmonic regurgitation, increase in intensity during inspiration.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DYNAMIC AUSCULTATION

items.question: Which maneuver can help distinguish the murmur of AS from MR by increasing systemic vascular resistance?
options: Sustained handgrip or blood pressure cuff inflation.
items.explanation: Murmurs associated with AS become softer, whereas those due to MR increase in intensity after maneuvers that raise systemic vascular resistance such as sustained handgrip or blood pressure cuff inflation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DYNAMIC AUSCULTATION

items.question: What is the effect of squatting on the murmur associated with hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM)?
options: The intensity decreases, but the duration increases.
There is no change in intensity or duration.
items.explanation: Squatting results in increased venous return and left ventricular afterload, which causes a decrease in both the intensity and duration of the murmur associated with HOCM.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DYNAMIC AUSCULTATION

items.question: Which clinical scenario would most likely not require further evaluation with echocardiography?
options: A new systolic murmur in a patient with a recent myocardial infarction and a marked fall in blood pressure.
An isolated grade 1 or 2 midsystolic murmur at the left sternal border in a healthy, active, and asymptomatic young adult.
A patient with suspected infective endocarditis presenting with fever, chills, anorexia, fatigue, dyspnea, splenomegaly, petechiae, and positive blood cultures.
A patient with early or late systolic murmurs.
items.explanation: An isolated grade 1 or 2 midsystolic murmur in a healthy, active, and asymptomatic young adult is likely benign and does not require further evaluation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · THE CLINICAL CONTEXT | ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY

items.question: When is echocardiography indicated for patients with midsystolic murmurs?
options: For all grade 1 or 2 midsystolic murmurs.
Only when accompanied by symptoms or signs of cardiovascular disease from other studies.
For any patient regardless of murmur grade.
Never, as it is not useful for midsystolic murmurs.
items.explanation: Echocardiography is indicated for patients with grade 1 or 2 midsystolic murmurs if there are other symptoms or signs of cardiovascular disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · THE CLINICAL CONTEXT | ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY

items.question: Which type of murmur is an indication for echocardiography?
options: Grade 1 midsystolic murmur in a patient with no other symptoms.
Continuous murmurs due to venous hum or mammary souffle.
items.explanation: Echocardiography is indicated for any patient with a diastolic murmur and continuous murmurs not due to a venous hum or mammary souffle.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · THE CLINICAL CONTEXT | ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY

items.question: What is the role of serial echocardiography in asymptomatic individuals with valvular heart disease?
options: It is not recommended as it provides no valuable information.
To follow the course and provide information impacting surgical timing decisions.
Only for initial diagnosis, not for longitudinal assessment.
Routine use to monitor all patients regardless of symptoms.
items.explanation: Serial echocardiography is used in asymptomatic individuals with valvular heart disease to follow their course and provide information that may impact surgical timing decisions.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · THE CLINICAL CONTEXT | ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY

items.question: Which imaging modality has largely supplanted the need for cardiac catheterization in patients with discrepancies between clinical and echocardiographic findings of regurgitant heart valve disease?
options: Electrocardiographically gated cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR) imaging
items.explanation: CMR imaging provides comprehensive quantitative information and has largely replaced the need for invasive assessments in certain cases.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · OTHER CARDIAC TESTING | INTEGRATED APPROACH

items.question: When is transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) particularly considered for further evaluation of a heart murmur?
options: When TTE windows are limited by body size, chest configuration, or intrathoracic pathology
In all patients with a heart murmur
Only when CMR imaging is unavailable
For routine screening of heart murmurs
items.explanation: TEE is considered when TTE windows are limited by factors such as body size, chest configuration, or intrathoracic pathology.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · OTHER CARDIAC TESTING | INTEGRATED APPROACH

items.question: What can both CMR and cardiac CT provide in the assessment of aortic valve leaflets?
options: Assessment of whether the valve is bi- or tricuspid
Detailed information on coronary artery disease
items.explanation: Both CMR and cardiac CT can assess if an aortic valve is bi- or tricuspid when TTE is uncertain.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · OTHER CARDIAC TESTING | INTEGRATED APPROACH

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)
items.statement: Diastolic murmurs begin after the first heart sound and end before the second heart sound.
items.explanation: Diastolic murmurs begin with or after S2 and end at or before S1.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)
items.statement: The Gallavardin effect refers to the higher pitch of the systolic murmur of aortic stenosis heard at the apex.
items.explanation: The Gallavardin effect describes how the coarse systolic murmur of aortic stenosis sounds higher pitched and more acoustically pure at the apex.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 2)
items.statement: A grade 3 murmur is accompanied by a palpable thrill over the site of maximal intensity.
items.explanation: A grade 3 murmur is loud but not accompanied by a palpable thrill; this occurs with a grade 4 murmur.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 2)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 2)
items.statement: High-intensity murmurs usually indicate significant structural heart disease.
items.explanation: Murmurs of grade 3 or greater intensity usually signify important structural heart disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 2)

items.statement: A new heart murmur after an MI always indicates acute mitral regurgitation.
items.explanation: A new murmur may indicate other complications such as ventricular septal rupture.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.statement: Tricuspid regurgitation with normal pulmonary artery pressures can produce an early systolic murmur.
items.explanation: TR with normal pulmonary artery pressures, as seen in infective endocarditis, may produce an early systolic murmur.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.statement: The murmur of aortic stenosis will decrease in intensity after a premature beat.
items.explanation: The murmur of aortic stenosis will increase in intensity or become louder in the beat after a premature beat.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.statement: A parasternal lift and ECG evidence of right ventricular hypertrophy indicate severe pressure overload in pulmonic stenosis.
items.explanation: Parasternal lift and ECG evidence of right ventricular hypertrophy are indicative of severe pressure overload in pulmonic stenosis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.statement: A grade 2 or 3 midsystolic murmur in patients with idiopathic dilation of the pulmonary artery is accompanied by a pulmonary ejection sound.
items.explanation: Patients with idiopathic dilation of the pulmonary artery may have a grade 2 or 3 midsystolic murmur and a pulmonary ejection sound.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 3)

items.statement: A late systolic murmur due to MVP is best heard at the left ventricular apex.
items.explanation: A late systolic murmur indicative of MVP is typically best heard at the left ventricular apex.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 3)

items.statement: The holosystolic murmur of tricuspid regurgitation is generally louder than that of mitral regurgitation.
items.explanation: The holosystolic murmur of chronic TR is generally softer than that of MR.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 4)

items.statement: A ventricular septal defect (VSD) murmur intensity does not change with inspiration.
items.explanation: The intensity of a VSD murmur remains unchanged with inspiration.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 4)

items.statement: The diastolic murmur due to mitral stenosis may increase with afterload reduction.
items.explanation: Afterload reduction can cause an increase in cardiac output and mitral valve flow, potentially increasing the intensity of a diastolic murmur from MS.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.statement: Peripheral signs of significant diastolic runoff are present in acute severe AR.
items.explanation: In the absence of heart failure, peripheral signs of significant diastolic runoff are generally not present in chronic severe AR.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.statement: Presystolic accentuation occurs in patients with atrial fibrillation.
items.explanation: Presystolic accentuation does not occur in patients with atrial fibrillation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.statement: The murmur of mitral stenosis is best heard using the diaphragm of the stethoscope.
items.explanation: The murmur of mitral stenosis is low-pitched and thus is best heard with the bell of the stethoscope.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.statement: A ruptured sinus of Valsalva aneurysm typically creates a continuous murmur at the upper right sternal border.
items.explanation: Rupture into a right heart chamber causes a continuous murmur at the upper right sternal border.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · CONTINUOUS MURMURS

items.statement: A cervical bruit with both systolic and diastolic components indicates a coronary arteriovenous fistula.
items.explanation: A cervical bruit with both components usually indicates high-grade carotid artery stenosis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · CONTINUOUS MURMURS

items.statement: The Valsalva maneuver typically increases the intensity of most heart murmurs.
items.explanation: Most murmurs decrease in intensity during the strain phase of the Valsalva maneuver, except for those associated with MVP and HOCM.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DYNAMIC AUSCULTATION

items.statement: The murmur of aortic stenosis (AS) becomes louder after a premature ventricular contraction.
items.explanation: Systolic murmurs due to left ventricular outflow obstruction, including AS, increase in intensity after a premature beat.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DYNAMIC AUSCULTATION

items.statement: Routine echocardiography is recommended for all asymptomatic patients with a grade 1 or 2 mid-systolic murmur.
items.explanation: Routine echocardiography is not recommended for asymptomatic patients with a grade 1 or 2 mid-systolic murmur without other signs of heart disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · THE CLINICAL CONTEXT | ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY

items.statement: Handheld cardiac ultrasound devices have completely replaced the stethoscope for all clinicians.
items.explanation: The use of handheld or miniaturized cardiac ultrasound devices has not been universally adopted and is highly operator-dependent.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · THE CLINICAL CONTEXT | ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY

items.statement: Coronary CT angiography is used to exclude coronary artery disease in patients with a high pretest probability of the disease before valve surgery.
items.explanation: Coronary CT angiography is used for patients with a low pretest probability of coronary artery disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · OTHER CARDIAC TESTING | INTEGRATED APPROACH

items.statement: Invasive angiography and hemodynamic assessment may be required for a more complete preoperative evaluation.
items.explanation: These assessments are sometimes necessary to provide comprehensive preoperative information.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · OTHER CARDIAC TESTING | INTEGRATED APPROACH

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)
items.assertion: The duration of a heart murmur depends on the length of time over which a pressure difference exists between two cardiac chambers.
items.reason: The magnitude and variability of this pressure difference, coupled with the geometry and compliance of the involved chambers or vessels, dictate the velocity of flow; the degree of turbulence; and the resulting frequency, configuration, and intensity of the murmur.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)
items.assertion: The murmur of mitral stenosis is heard as a high-frequency event.
items.reason: It is indicative of the left atrial-left ventricular diastolic pressure gradient.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the murmur of mitral stenosis is a low-frequency, rumbling sound.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 1)

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 2)
items.assertion: A murmur's intensity can be misleadingly soft when cardiac output is reduced significantly.
items.reason: The intensity of a heart murmur may be diminished by any process that increases the distance between the intracardiac source and the stethoscope on the chest wall.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason provided is not related to why a murmur's intensity can be misleadingly soft when cardiac output is reduced.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur (part 2)

items.assertion: Acute severe MR due to papillary muscle rupture often presents with a murmur.
items.reason: The murmur may be absent in up to 50% of cases.
items.explanation: While acute severe MR can present with a murmur, it may be absent in many cases.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.assertion: A congenital small muscular VSD is associated with signs of pulmonary hypertension.
items.reason: The defect closes progressively during septal contraction, and thus the murmur is confined to early systole.
items.explanation: Signs of pulmonary hypertension are absent in a congenital small muscular VSD.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.assertion: The murmur intensity of aortic stenosis varies directly with cardiac output.
items.reason: With normal cardiac output, the AS murmur is louder and suggests severe AS.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.assertion: The carotid pulse examination is most discriminatory in older patients with stiffened arteries.
items.reason: A small and delayed upstroke (parvus et tardus) is consistent with severe AS.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the carotid pulse examination is less discriminatory in older patients, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.assertion: Leaflet flail in mitral valve prolapse results in a murmur that can be heard throughout the precordium.
items.reason: Leaflet flail is associated with a murmur of grade 3 or 4 intensity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 3)

items.assertion: A late systolic murmur in acute myocardial ischemia is due to apical tethering.
items.reason: The intensity of this murmur decreases with increased left ventricular afterload.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; the intensity increases with increased afterload.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · SYSTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 3)

items.assertion: The Graham Steell murmur is associated with pulmonary hypertension.
items.reason: Pulmonic regurgitation most commonly results from dilation of the valve annulus due to chronic elevation of pulmonary artery pressure.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association between the Graham Steell murmur and pulmonary hypertension.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.assertion: An Austin Flint murmur can be distinguished from a mitral stenosis murmur by its response to afterload reduction.
items.reason: The Austin Flint murmur becomes shorter and softer with afterload reduction, while the intensity of a mitral stenosis murmur may increase or remain constant.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains how an Austin Flint murmur can be distinguished from a mitral stenosis murmur.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 1)

items.assertion: The intensity of a mitral stenosis murmur increases during exercise.
items.reason: Exercise increases cardiac output and mitral valve flow.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the intensity of the murmur increases during exercise.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.assertion: An opening snap is present in acute rheumatic fever.
items.reason: The opening snap is due to flow through an edematous mitral valve.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as the opening snap is not present during the acute phase of rheumatic fever; the reason is true but unrelated.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DIASTOLIC HEART MURMURS (part 2)

items.assertion: The continuous mammary souffle of pregnancy is louder in diastole.
items.reason: Firm pressure with the diaphragm of the stethoscope can eliminate the diastolic portion of the murmur.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the mammary souffle is louder in systole, and firm pressure eliminates the diastolic part.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · CONTINUOUS MURMURS

items.assertion: The murmur of mitral regurgitation (MR) increases in intensity with maneuvers that raise systemic vascular resistance.
items.reason: Increased systemic vascular resistance enhances the left ventricular-left atrial gradient, causing a louder MR murmur.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; increased systemic vascular resistance does cause an increase in the intensity of the MR murmur.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DYNAMIC AUSCULTATION

items.assertion: The murmur associated with hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (HOCM) becomes louder during squatting.
items.reason: Squatting decreases venous return and left ventricular afterload, leading to an increase in the intensity of the HOCM murmur.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the murmur associated with HOCM actually becomes softer during squatting due to increased venous return and left ventricular afterload.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · DYNAMIC AUSCULTATION

items.assertion: Echocardiography should be considered when there is a clinical need to verify normal cardiac structure in patients with noncardiac symptoms.
items.reason: The performance of serial echocardiography helps in the longitudinal assessment of asymptomatic individuals with valvular heart disease.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · THE CLINICAL CONTEXT | ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY

items.assertion: Doppler signals of trace or mild valvular regurgitation can be detected with structurally normal valves.
items.reason: Such signals are likely to generate enough turbulence to create an audible murmur.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; these Doppler signals do not typically generate enough turbulence for an audible murmur.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · THE CLINICAL CONTEXT | ECHOCARDIOGRAPHY

items.assertion: Cardiac auscultation using a stethoscope remains essential in medicine.
items.reason: It provides benefits beyond accurate recognition of heart sounds, such as improving diagnostic accuracy with newer technologies.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with a Heart Murmur · OTHER CARDIAC TESTING | INTEGRATED APPROACH
